# Hiking Summary — 2026-06-17

**Generated by:** hike-izer Skill (CARD-0073)
**Data source:** JCTsh Environmental Data Google Sheets (Environmental Data, Hiking Observations, GPS Track), via `action=export`, filtered to `source=hiking-monitor`
**Note:** this hike's GPS session starts late on June 17 and runs past midnight into June 18 — attributed here to June 17 (the day it started), per Hike-izer's day-scoping rule. Its own separate day, June 18, has its own hike and its own summary (`2026-06-18_hike-summary.md`) — a genuinely different, shorter, morning hike, not a continuation of this one.

---

## The Hike

This was an evening push into the night — the hike started at 23:51 UTC (roughly 4:51 PM local) with the sun already well into its western descent, and didn't wrap up until nearly 3 AM UTC, by which point the sun had set and the last stretch was covered in darkness. Elevation moved around rather than trending one direction — some real climbing and descending over the course of the route, not a flat walk.

The device's own logging had a rough patch beforehand: a ten-hour stretch with no readings at all earlier in the day, right up until the hike itself began — most likely the device was off, packed away, or out of range for that stretch rather than actively logging and simply not producing data. Once the hike started, though, logging picked back up and stayed reasonably consistent through the night. Conditions were hot into the evening and eased off as night fell; battery and UV both look normal for this stage of the trip (see the LTR-390 caveat below).

No voice observations were recorded on this hike.

---

## Expected vs. Actual Data Coverage — Pipeline Health Check

Environmental Data: 423 hiking-monitor readings against 720 expected at the standard cadence for a full day (58.8% coverage) — the shortfall is almost entirely explained by the ten-hour pre-hike silent stretch (13:56–23:57 UTC) rather than gaps during the hike itself, which logged fairly steadily once underway. GPS correlation matched 385 of the 423 readings (91.0%) to a track point — a healthy rate.

UV index staying at essentially zero through a hot, sun-exposed evening hike is not physically credible and lines up with the LTR-390 sensor issue already tracked in CARD-0065 — treat this reading as suspect, not as a real "no UV" condition.

**Bottom line:** the pipeline itself worked well during the hike; the ten-hour pre-hike gap is the one thing worth a second look, and the UV reading remains a known, separate issue rather than a new finding.
